results.possibleIdentity: James "Jim" Pigg was a software engineer and retired military officer who was active during the late 1960s and 1970s. He graduated from West Point in 1953 and served in the Signal Corps for over 21 years, retiring with the rank of lieutenant colonel. Following his military career, he joined IBM as a software engineer. Given that Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) frequently collaborated with engineers from IBM and Bell Laboratories during the late 1960s and early 1970s, he represents a likely historical identity of a technical collaborator from that era, though no specific artistic works or E.A.T. project credits are linked to him in common web databases.

Jim Pigg is a name widely recognized in the art world as a character from the sporting novels of R.S. Surtees (1805–1864), particularly 'Handley Cross'. This character was a North Country huntsman and servant to John Jorrocks. He was the subject of numerous historical artistic prints and illustrations, most notably those by the sporting artist Charles Johnson Payne (known as 'Snaffles') and the caricaturist John Leech. While this is a fictional character, the name 'Jim Pigg' appears in historical art records and exhibition catalogs (such as those for the artist Snaffles) that were active or circulating in the mid-20th century.

results.possibleIdentity: Rosemary Beresford is a New Zealand-based theatre practitioner, academic, and pharmacist who has been a prominent figure in the Dunedin arts and scientific communities since at least the late 1960s. She is often described as a "stalwart" of the Globe Theatre in Dunedin, where she and her husband, Brian Beresford, served as long-term committee members and contributed to experimental and avant-garde productions during the 1960s, 1970s, and beyond. Professionally, she is a distinguished scientist (Dr. Rosemary Beresford, ONZM) who served as an Associate Professor and Associate Dean at the University of Otago's School of Pharmacy. Her work in the history of pharmacy and traditional medicine (Rongoā Māori) aligns with the interdisciplinary goals of groups like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which promoted collaborations between artists and scientists. She was also a primary contact for the Dunedin presentation of the E.A.T. exhibition "Sensing the Future" at the Dunedin Public Art Gallery in 2014.

results.possibleIdentity: Stephanie Evanitsky (likely the person referred to as Stephanie Evansky) is an artist, choreographer, and a pioneer of aerial dance who was prominently active in the late 1960s and 1970s. In 1969, she founded the Multigravitational Aerodance Group, one of the first dance companies dedicated to performing in the air using suspension systems, ropes, and scaffolding. Her work sought to create illusions of weightlessness and explored the human body's movement in zero-gravity-like environments, influenced by the postmodern dance movement and the multimedia experiments of Alwin Nikolais. Evanitsky's artistic career is historically linked to the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) milieu; her records and grant applications are archived alongside E.A.T. documentation in the Jerome Robbins Papers at the New York Public Library, reflecting her involvement in the cross-disciplinary scene of that era. She was awarded a Guggenheim Fellowship in 1974 and presented major works at venues such as the Guggenheim Museum and the O.K. Harris Gallery in New York City. Notable choreographic works include 'Splatt' (1972) and 'Buff Her Blind' (1974).

results.possibleIdentity: Elizabeth "Betty" Voelker (1931–c. 2000s) was an internationally recognized American artist and colorist who also worked under the name "Hera" starting in 1977. Born in 1931, with biographical ties to both Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, and New Orleans, Louisiana, she received her Bachelor of Fine Arts in 1953 from the Carnegie Institute of Technology (now Carnegie Mellon University), where she studied under Samuel Rosenberg. She was active in the Pittsburgh art scene and the Associated Artists of Pittsburgh before moving to San Francisco in 1958. Her career in California included lecturing for various museums and the University of California between 1972 and 1987. Voelker's artistic output spanned abstract nature paintings, formal collages, and later, participatory "tent forms" and environmental installations such as "Sweet Skirt" and "Death Singer," which were featured in the 1977 exhibition "Locations" at the Rose Art Museum. She was a recipient of three Pollock-Krasner Foundation grants, a National Endowment for the Arts grant to establish prison arts programming, and a Rockefeller Foundation grant for residency at the Bellagio Study and Conference Center in Italy. Other prestigious residencies included the American Academy in Rome and the Djerassi Foundation. Her work is held in major public collections, including the Smithsonian's National Museum of American Art, the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art, the Santa Barbara Museum of Art, and the Carnegie Museum of Art. She is also documented in the Political Art Documentation/Distribution (PAD/D) archive at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) and the Giuseppe Panza papers at the Getty Research Institute, which highlight her contributions to environmental and conceptual art during the late 1960s and 1970s.

results.possibleIdentity: Joan Willets Price was an artist and administrator active in the late 1960s and 1970s, particularly known for her role within the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. She served as the Membership Director (and sometimes identified as an Executive Secretary or Administrator) for E.A.T., a non-profit organization founded in 1966 by Billy Klüver, Robert Rauschenberg, Robert Whitman, and Fred Waldhauer to facilitate collaborations between artists and engineers. Price was a central figure in managing the organization's extensive membership, which grew to thousands of participants globally. She was involved in major E.A.T. initiatives, including the development of the Pepsi Pavilion for Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan. Beyond her administrative work, she was an active artist in the New Jersey and New York area; in 1975, she served as a judge for the annual art show in Union County, New Jersey. She is sometimes referred to as Joan Price or Joan W. Price in professional contexts.

wikidata_candidates.reasoning: The name 'Sari Dienes' is an exact match. The Wikidata entity describes a visual artist who lived from 1898 to 1992, which perfectly aligns with the 1970 date on the card and the provided context that the person was an artist active during that period.

results.possibleIdentity: Sari Dienes (1898–1992) was a Hungarian-born American experimental artist whose career spanned six decades and linked European Surrealism with American Neo-Dada and Pop art. She is best known for her 'Sidewalk Rubbings'—large-scale frottage works created by taking impressions of urban textures such as manhole covers and subway gratings using ink and a medical fabric called Webril. Active throughout the late 1960s and 1970s, she was a central figure in the New York avant-garde, serving as a mentor and major influence on younger artists like Robert Rauschenberg and Jasper Johns. In 1961, she moved to the Gate Hill Cooperative ('The Land') in Stony Point, New York, where she lived alongside collaborators such as John Cage, Merce Cunningham, David Tudor, and Stan VanDerBeek. Dienes was directly associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.); her project proposals and correspondence are preserved in the organization's official records at the Getty Research Institute. During the 1970s, she was a founding member of the feminist A.I.R. Gallery (1972) and helped establish the Ear Inn (1977), a hub for experimental performance. Her work also encompassed assemblage, ceramics, sound installations, and performance art, including her 'Snow Paintings' documented by photographer Peter Moore.

results.possibleIdentity: Oliver Gordon Selfridge (1926–2008) was a British-born American computer scientist and mathematician, widely regarded as a pioneer of artificial intelligence and machine perception. While primarily known for his scientific contributions—most notably the 'Pandemonium' architecture (1959)—he was a significant figure in the intersection of technology and the arts during the late 1960s and early 1970s. During his tenure at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) and Bolt, Beranek and Newman (BBN), Selfridge became an intellectual collaborator and consultant for influential art theorist Jack Burnham. Burnham, a proponent of 'Systems Art' and curator of the landmark 1970 exhibition 'Software' at the Jewish Museum, aligned himself with Selfridge and computer scientist Jack Nolan to develop his theories on the aesthetics of intelligent systems. This collaboration occurred while Burnham was a fellow at MIT’s Center for Advanced Visual Studies (CAVS). Selfridge's work on machine learning and pattern recognition was foundational to the 'cybernetic art' movement and was discussed alongside organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which promoted similar cross-disciplinary collaborations. Additionally, Selfridge had a direct creative output as the author of several children's books, including 'Sticks,' 'Fingers Come In Fives,' 'All About Mud,' and 'Trouble With Dragons.'
